quiz.title: Unit 8 Quiz 6, Grade 10 Biology
quiz.subtitle: Virology: Transmission, Vaccines, and Viral Pathogenesis
quiz.subject: Biology
quiz.topic: Plant Virus Transmission, Vaccine Mechanism, Vector-Borne Viruses, Viral Components, Retroviruses, HIV, CD4 Receptors, Herpesvirus Latency

questions.question: Viral diseases in plants are often transmitted by:
questions.explanation: Plant viruses are commonly transmitted by insects (vectors) like aphids, leafhoppers, and beetles that feed on plants. They can also be transmitted through seeds, grafting, or mechanical damage, but insects are the primary vectors.

questions.question: Vaccines work against viruses by:
options: Killing viruses directly in the body
questions.explanation: Vaccines stimulate the immune system to produce memory cells and antibodies without causing disease. This prepares the body to respond rapidly upon actual infection. Vaccines do not directly kill viruses or remove viral DNA.

questions.question: Which of the following viruses is transmitted by mosquitoes?
questions.explanation: Dengue virus is transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes. HIV is transmitted through bodily fluids, influenza through airborne droplets, and Hepatitis B through blood and bodily fluids.

questions.question: Which of the following is true about viruses?
options: They require a host cell to reproduce
questions.explanation: Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites that require a host cell to reproduce. They are acellular (not cellular), lack ribosomes and other organelles, and cannot carry out independent metabolism. They hijack host cell machinery for replication.

questions.question: Which can reproduce by binary fission?
questions.explanation: Bacteria reproduce asexually by binary fission, where one cell divides into two identical daughter cells. Viruses are acellular and cannot reproduce independently—they require host cell machinery to replicate. Binary fission does not occur in viruses.

questions.question: Which uses lytic or lysogenic cycles for replication?
questions.explanation: Viruses, particularly bacteriophages, use lytic and lysogenic cycles for replication. In the lytic cycle, viruses replicate and immediately cause host cell lysis. In the lysogenic cycle, viral DNA integrates into the host genome (as a prophage) and replicates with the host without causing immediate cell death. Bacteria reproduce by binary fission, not by these cycles.

questions.question: Which can survive independently in the environment?
questions.explanation: Bacteria are cellular organisms that can survive independently in the environment. They have metabolic pathways and can reproduce without a host. Viruses are acellular and cannot survive or replicate independently—they require a living host cell to reproduce. Outside a host, viruses may remain inert but cannot carry out any life processes.

questions.question: Vaccines are used to prevent:
questions.explanation: Vaccines are used to prevent both bacterial and viral infections. Examples include: viral vaccines (measles, mumps, rubella, influenza, COVID-19) and bacterial vaccines (tetanus, diphtheria, pertussis, tuberculosis - BCG). Vaccines work by stimulating the immune system to produce memory cells and antibodies against specific pathogens.

questions.question: Which can form spores to survive harsh conditions?
questions.explanation: Certain bacteria (like Bacillus and Clostridium) can form endospores—highly resistant dormant structures that survive extreme heat, drought, radiation, and chemicals. Viruses cannot form spores; they may remain inert outside a host but do not have specialized survival structures like bacterial endospores.

questions.question: Which can mutate rapidly leading to new strains?
questions.explanation: Both viruses and bacteria can mutate rapidly, leading to new strains. Viruses, especially RNA viruses like influenza and coronaviruses, mutate quickly due to error-prone replication. Bacteria can mutate and also acquire new genes through horizontal transfer (plasmids, transformation, transduction), leading to antibiotic-resistant strains and new variants.

questions.question: Which can be visualized under a light microscope easily?
questions.explanation: Bacteria are typically 1-10 micrometers in size and can be easily visualized under a light microscope with appropriate staining (like Gram stain). Viruses are much smaller (20-300 nanometers) and require electron microscopes to be visualized, as they are below the resolution limit of light microscopes.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of all viruses?
questions.explanation: Not all viruses have envelopes (e.g., adenoviruses, parvoviruses are naked). All viruses have a capsid (protein coat), nucleic acid genome (DNA or RNA), and proteins. Envelopes are derived from host membranes and found only in some viruses.

questions.question: Reverse transcriptase is an essential enzyme for replication of:
questions.explanation: HIV is a retrovirus that uses reverse transcriptase to convert its RNA genome into DNA, which then integrates into the host genome. Influenza is an RNA virus but not a retrovirus, herpesviruses are DNA viruses, and Hepatitis A is an RNA virus.

questions.question: The HIV receptor CD4 is primarily expressed on:
questions.explanation: CD4 is the hallmark surface marker of helper T cells. HIV targets and destroys these cells, leading to immunodeficiency. Cytotoxic T cells express CD8, B cells express CD19/20, and NK cells express CD16/56.

questions.question: Which mechanism allows latent herpesviruses to evade host immune detection?
options: Integration of viral DNA into host chromosomes
Formation of episomal DNA in nucleus without active replication
Destruction of MHC class I molecules
questions.explanation: Latent herpesviruses (HSV, VZV, EBV) maintain their genome as episomal DNA in the nucleus with limited gene expression, avoiding immune detection. They do not integrate into host chromosomes (like retroviruses). Rapid mutation is typical of influenza, and MHC destruction occurs in some adenoviruses.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered viral transmission and pathogenesis! 🦠
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the differences between enveloped and non-enveloped viruses.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on retrovirus mechanisms and HIV targets.
scoreMessages.0: Please review virus transmission routes, vaccine function, and viral latency.
